directions.name: Use The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling As The Next Control Path
directions.hypothesis: Round 9/17 showed that reopening the PTX prefetch family on top of the new export base does improve the failed round-8 variant, but it still cannot beat the accepted base because it only trades long-scoreboard down to 6.58 for a higher barrier cost of 6.68. That effectively closes the reopened PTX prefetch family. The best next move is now the non-PTX 128x128 sibling control: it keeps the same hot-band tile geometry while removing the PTX-specific export/store and refill interaction that is now dominating the remaining tradeoff.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX-specific export/store and refill interaction versus the simpler non-PTX 128x128 sibling path.
directions.risk: Low to medium. This is a bounded control-path switch that preserves the same 128x128 shape, but it may simply underperform the accepted PTX base if the remaining cost is not PTX-specific.
directions.stop_condition: Stop this control path immediately if it is both slower than 24.84582424 ms and no cleaner in the profiler than the accepted PTX base.

directions.name: Reopen The Measured 64x384 Fixed-Main-Tile Control Path
directions.hypothesis: The older `64x384` fixed-main-tile sweep is still the only broader alternate family in repo history with explicit measured ranking. It remains behind the 128x128 sibling because its evidence is older and far broader than the current PTX baseline, but it is still the main fallback if the 128x128 control path also stalls.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Broader hot-band path selection and arithmetic-intensity tradeoff rather than PTX helper overhead.
directions.risk: Medium. The family has measured ancestry, but it is a much broader branch change than the accepted PTX path and can easily give back recent gains.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if the 64x384 control path is materially slower than the accepted PTX base or clearly reopens a broad-family regression.

directions.name: Retry Grouping / Orchestration Only If Control Paths Stall
directions.hypothesis: The grouped-row window retune lost on the older base, but it was measured before the new accepted export cleanup lowered the memory side of the PTX path. It still does not outrank the two control paths, yet it remains the last bounded PTX-adjacent family once deeper export cleanup, expanded B-shared skew, and reopened prefetch have all been closed.
directions.expected_bottleneck: CTA grouping and orchestration overhead around the PTX hot-band grouped-row mapping.
directions.risk: Medium. The family has already posted one measured loss, so it should stay tertiary until the cleaner control paths are exhausted.
directions.stop_condition: Only revisit this family after the higher-ranked controls stall, and close it again immediately if it remains slower than the accepted PTX base.

notes: Round 10/17 diagnosis for run 20260420_162425_bf16_gemm_v1_bbcc928. Human-review mapping for this round: keep the deeper export cleanup from round 8 closed, and now also close the reopened PTX prefetch-handoff family after round 9/17. The new evidence is that the narrower future-refill retime reduced long-scoreboard to 6.58 and DRAM to 11.44, but it still lost to the accepted base because barrier rose to 6.68 and total runtime stayed at 25.67678452 ms. No new explicit human idea family is queued in state/human_review.md, so the ranking now shifts away from more PTX micro-adjustments: accept the non-PTX 128x128 sibling as the next control path, defer the older-but-measured 64x384 control as the broader fallback, and keep grouping/orchestration only as a tertiary PTX-adjacent retry if the cleaner controls also stall.
